update: Tulsa City Council voted UNANIMOUSLY on March 25, 2026 to approve a 9-month moratorium on new data center construction (amended down from the original 365-day proposal). Councilor Laura Bellis reduced the duration to 9 months as a compromise after pushback in a committee meeting. The moratorium runs through Dec. 31, 2026, or until zoning rules are updated. Exemptions: Phase 1 and 2 of Project Anthem (east Tulsa) and Project Clydesdale (north Tulsa, already under construction). 19 people spoke in favor during public comment.

The Champaign County ZBA voted to ADVANCE the proposed 12-month moratorium on data centers with 10,000+ sq ft processing area. The measure now goes to the Environmental Land Use Committee (potentially April 9) before a full County Board vote later in April. A Data Center Activities Task Force has been established to develop zoning and permitting standards for unincorporated land, with protecting local water resources as a key focus. Full county board has NOT yet voted.

Sanders-AOC AI Data Center Moratorium Act officially INTRODUCED on March 25, 2026. Received massive media coverage (PBS, Rolling Stone, Axios, WaPo, Common Dreams, Democracy Now). Food & Water Watch endorsed and called on other members to co-sponsor. Industry opposition swift: Center for Data Innovation called it unjustified; Sen. John Fetterman (D-PA) rejected it, agreeing with Interior Sec. Doug Burgum that a moratorium is a 'surrender flag' to China. No named co-sponsors beyond Sanders and AOC found. Extremely unlikely to advance under Republican Congress. At least 63 local moratorium actions cited; 12+ state moratorium bills filed this cycle.

Governor Larry Rhoden SIGNED SB 135 into law on March 25, 2026. Key provisions now in effect: (1) data centers using 10+ MW must pay for their own power infrastructure, (2) 10+ MW facilities must obey water usage limits set by the Board of Water Management to protect residential and essential public service water supplies, (3) semi-annual water usage reports required, (4) explicit protection of local government authority to regulate, limit, or prohibit data centers. Context: incentive bills and moratorium bills all failed in Pierre; SB 135 was the only data center bill to pass.

Oregon legislature passed HB 4084 with the data center tax break moratorium amendment intact before sine die on March 9. The amendment blocks new data center projects from Enterprise Zone property tax break eligibility until summer 2027. Governor Kotek's office expressed support, saying it will 'address immediate concerns.' As of March 28, the bill is on the governor's desk but no confirmation of formal signing found yet. The 2026 short session is fully adjourned.

ESSB 6231 passed the legislature and is part of the supplemental budget now on Governor Ferguson's desk. The bill repeals the sales tax exemption for data center equipment refurbishment/replacement at existing sites while preserving the exemption for new facilities. Revenue estimate: $63.1M current biennium, $143.9M for 2027-29 period. Ferguson requested the bill via OFM. No confirmation of formal signature yet as of March 28, but Ferguson is expected to sign as it was his proposal. Republicans warned the repeal could push data centers to competitor states.

No change since March 23 tabling. The Sangamon County Board voted 15-13 on March 23 to TABLE the CyrusOne zoning proposal after 700+ residents attended and hours of opposition testimony. The $500M, 636 MW project on 280 acres of Talkington Township farmland remains in limbo. No new vote date has been scheduled as of March 28.

Joliet City Council voted 8-1 on March 20, 2026 to APPROVE the 795-acre data center campus ('Joliet Technology Center'). District 5 Councilwoman Suzanna Ibarra was the lone no vote. Project by HW Technology Park Development LLC: 24 two-story buildings, 4 phases over 5-7 years, near Chicagoland Speedway. Expected to generate $677M in property taxes for Joliet Township High School District 204, 7,000-10,000 construction jobs, 700 permanent jobs. Target operational by 2030. Bisnow reported $20B total investment figure.

Aurora City Council voted on March 24, 2026 to APPROVE new data center regulations described as 'the most robust throughout the State of Illinois and some of the most protective in the country.' Key rules: 56 dB daytime / 46 dB nighttime noise caps, generators and chillers must be 1,500 ft from homes, City Council approval required for any new facility, mandatory sound/noise/water studies before development, vibration and energy use standards. Rules apply only to new centers, not the 4 existing or 5 already in the pipeline. The city's moratorium was extended 30 days before these rules took effect.

NEW ENTRY: Data Center Watch report (released ~March 25-27, 2026) quantifies the national impact of community opposition: $64 billion in U.S. data center projects blocked ($18B) or delayed ($46B) over the past two years. At least 142 activist groups across 24 states organizing against data centers. Notable blocked projects include the $24.7B PW Digital Gateway (QTS/Compass, contested in court) and Tract's $14B Arizona West Valley project (withdrawn after rezoning denied). Report was widely cited by The Hill, Data Center Dynamics, and others.

NEW ENTRY: Mountain West moratorium roundup (KUNR, March 27, 2026). Kootenai County, Idaho lifted its moratorium after banning data centers over the aquifer and requiring conditional use permits. Logan County, Colorado lifted its moratorium after passing regulations. Article contextualizes the Sanders-AOC federal moratorium bill alongside local/county actions in the Mountain West, noting bipartisan support for data center regulation in traditionally conservative rural areas.

Fayetteville developer officially WITHDREW the appeal on March 18, removing the item from the council agenda. This follows the March 5 UDO changes that eliminated data centers as a permitted use in all zoning categories. The 'Power of the People' headline from The Citizen marked this as a community victory.

No new developments since the initial withdrawal. JRE Strategies pulled the application before the March 16 city commission vote after the planning commission's 7-0 rejection. The Noble Foundation and neighborhood residents had voiced strong opposition over noise and power concerns.
No new developments since March 5 withdrawal by Natelli Investments. The related Apex moratorium entry (apex-moratorium-nc-2026) should be checked for any council vote updates.
